44: A fulfillment: the LORD said to my Lord, sit at my right hand until I make your enemies a footstool for your feet. Jesus's use of Psalms 110:1 reveals that the Messiah's identity exceeds Davidic sonship — he is David's Lord, which means he is the divine Son who pre-exists his own Davidic lineage. The Son of Man coming on the clouds (Matthew 24:30) is seated at God's right hand (Psalms 110:1) while his enemies are being subdued — the enthronement is present and active, not merely future. Every exercise of Christ's authority in Matthew (healing, teaching, forgiving, commissioning) is the exercise of this right-hand authority from the beginning of his ministry.
1: A revelation of God: "And again Jesus spoke to them in parables, saying..." The continued parabolic address to the chief priests and Pharisees links the Wedding Banquet parable to the Wicked Tenants. The Christological frame: Jesus keeps teaching the religious authorities through parables — giving them opportunity to hear, while the form itself reveals their inability to receive.
2: A direct revelation: "The kingdom of heaven may be compared to a king who gave a wedding feast for his son." The Wedding Banquet parable is the most explicit allegory in the parables: the king is the Father, the son is the Son (Jesus), and the wedding feast is the eschatological celebration of the kingdom. The Christological identification: the entire history of salvation is the Father's preparation of the wedding feast to honor his Son. Rejecting the invitation is rejecting the Son whom the feast honors.
3: A revelation of God: "And sent his servants to call those who were invited to the wedding feast, but they were not willing to come." The first sending — to those already invited (Israel, particularly the religious leadership) — is refused. "Not willing to come" (ouk ēthelon elthein) is the volitional refusal: not inability but unwillingness. The Christological bearing: the kingdom's invitation to those with the most preparation and expectation is the first and most culpable refusal.
4: A revelation of God: "Again he sent other servants, saying, 'Tell those who are invited, 'See, I have prepared my dinner, my oxen and my fat calves have been slaughtered, and everything is ready. Come to the wedding feast.''" The repeated, urgent invitation — "everything is ready" — is the urgency of the gospel: the preparation is complete, the feast is set, only the guests' presence is lacking. The Christological urgency: the Son has come, the sacrifice has been made (the oxen and fat calves anticipate the cross), and the invitation is extended again.
5: A revelation of God: "But they paid no attention and went off, one to his farm, another to his business." The indifference to the royal invitation — turning to ordinary business rather than the wedding — is the Christological diagnosis of the thorn-soil: "cares of the world and deceitfulness of riches choke the word" (Matthew 13:22). The farm and business are not evil; the Christological problem is their priority over the kingdom's invitation. Ordinary life becomes the refusal of eschatological grace.
6: A revelation of God: "While the rest seized his servants, treated them shamefully, and killed them." The violence against the servants mirrors the Wicked Tenants parable: the progression from indifference (Matthew 22:5) to active hostility. The Christological pattern: rejection of the kingdom invitation escalates from inattention to persecution. The servants killed are the prophets and the apostles who bore the invitation.
7: A revelation of God: "The king was angry, and he sent his troops and destroyed those murderers and burned their city." The burning of the city by the king's troops is a transparent reference to Jerusalem's destruction in 70 AD — the eschatological consequence of rejecting the Son's invitation. The Christological judgment: the murder of the servants and the rejection of the Son's wedding feast bring the judgment that Jesus predicted throughout the discourse (Matthew 23:37-38; Matthew 24:1-2).
8: A revelation of God: "Then he said to his servants, 'The wedding feast is ready, but those who were invited were not worthy.'" The unworthiness of the first-invited guests is not a fixed characteristic but the verdict on their refusal. The Christological inversion: those who seemed most worthy (the religious leaders, those long-prepared) proved unworthy by their refusal; the unworthy (Gentiles, sinners, outsiders) will be made worthy by accepting the invitation.
9: A revelation of God: "Go therefore to the main roads and invite to the wedding feast as many as you find." The going to the main roads — the broad roads where all kinds of people travel — is the Christological Great Commission in parabolic form. The invitation goes to everyone regardless of preparation or qualification. The "therefore" connects the new mission to the rejection of the first: because the first-invited refused, the whole world becomes the field of invitation.
10: A revelation of God: "And those servants went out into the roads and gathered all whom they found, both bad and good. So the wedding hall was filled with guests." The mixed assembly — bad and good — is the Christological reality of the present kingdom: both wheat and weeds (Matthew 13:24-30), both good fish and bad (Matthew 13:47-50). The full wedding hall represents the church's present mixed condition. The final separation (Matthew 22:11-13) is the eschatological judgment that distinguishes genuine from false response.
11: A revelation of God: "But when the king came in to look at the guests, he saw there a man who had no wedding garment." The man without a wedding garment represents those who enter the community of the called without the garment of genuine righteousness — the "putting on Christ" (Romans 13:14) that constitutes genuine response. The Christological bearing: entry into the feast-community is necessary but not sufficient; the garment of new life is required. The wedding garment is not earned but given — refusing it is the second form of refusal.
12: A revelation of God: "And he said to him, 'Friend, how did you get in here without a wedding garment?' And he was speechless." The "Friend" (hetaire) is the same address as the workers (Matthew 20:13) and Judas (Matthew 26:50) — the address to those who are in proximity but not in genuine relationship. The speechlessness before the king is the Christological image of the judgment day when no defense is possible (cf. Romans 3:19: "every mouth may be stopped"). The one without the garment has nothing to say.
13: A revelation of God: "Then the king said to the attendants, 'Bind him hand and foot and cast him into the outer darkness. In that place there will be weeping and gnashing of teeth.'" The outer darkness and weeping — the seventh occurrence of this formula in Matthew (Matthew 8:12; Matthew 13:42, 50; Matthew 22:13; Matthew 24:51; Matthew 25:30) — is the Christological refrain of eschatological exclusion. Those who refuse the invitation and those who enter without the garment share the same end: exclusion from the feast. The kingdom's joy and the judgment's darkness are the two destinations.
14: A direct revelation: "For many are called, but few are chosen." The closing maxim of the Wedding Banquet parable is the Christological summary of election and response: the call goes to all ("many"), but the chosen are those who receive it genuinely. The Christological tension: the invitation is universal (go to all the roads), but not all who hear it receive it with the genuine response that the garment represents. Election is not hostility to the un-chosen but the fulfillment of the grace genuinely received.
15: A revelation of God: "Then the Pharisees went and plotted how to entangle him in his talk." The conspiracy to trap Jesus in his speech is the Christological confrontation with hostile testing. The three tests that follow (Caesar, resurrection, greatest commandment) are political, theological, and legal traps designed to make Jesus choose a position that alienates some constituency. The one who speaks only truth answers each trap without being caught.
16: A revelation of God: "And they sent their disciples to him, along with the Herodians, saying, 'Teacher, we know that you are true and teach the way of God truthfully, and you do not care about anyone's opinion, for you are not swayed by appearances.'" The flattery before the trap is the Christological test of vanity: will Jesus be moved by praise? The affirmations they make — true, teaches God's way, not swayed by appearances — are all accurate descriptions of the Son of God. The trap is set inside a genuine description of who he is.
17: A revelation of God: "Tell us, then, what you think. Is it lawful to pay taxes to Caesar, or not?" The Caesar-question is a political dilemma: say yes, alienate the nationalists; say no, invite Roman charges of sedition. The Christological response will transcend the binary by reframing the question at a higher level — not which human authority to obey but what belongs to whom.
18: A revelation of God: "But Jesus, aware of their malice, said, 'Why put me to the test, you hypocrites?'" The awareness of their malice — divine perception of hidden motive — is the Christological knowledge of the human heart (cf. Matthew 9:4; Matthew 12:25). The naming of the test as malice is the prophetic unmasking: behind the flattery is hostility. The "hypocrites" charge names the gap between the flattering words and the malicious intent.
19: A revelation of God: "Show me the coin for the tax.' And they brought him a denarius." The coin produced belongs to the tribute money — a Roman denarius bearing the emperor's image and inscription. The Christological economy: Jesus does not refuse the coin or its use; he uses it to draw the distinction between imperial and divine claims.
20: A revelation of God: "And Jesus said to them, 'Whose likeness and inscription is this?' They said, 'Caesar's.'" The image and inscription on the coin are the tokens of Caesar's ownership-claim. The Christological counterpart: human beings bear the image of God (eikōn tou theou, Genesis 1:26-27) — the inscription of God's ownership. The coin with Caesar's image belongs to Caesar; the person with God's image belongs to God.
21: A direct revelation: "He said to them, 'Therefore render to Caesar the things that are Caesar's, and to God the things that are God's.' When they heard it, they marveled and left him and went away." The "render to God what is God's" is the Christological trump card: everything ultimately belongs to God, including Caesar himself. The astonishment of the questioners is the recognition that they have been out-argued at the deepest level — the question about taxes has become a question about the image of God in every human being. The trap failed.
22: A revelation of God: "When they heard it, they marveled and left him and went away." The departure of the Pharisees and Herodians — without a further word — is the Christological victory in the controversy. They came with a trap; they leave with the trap unanswered in their hands. The one who answers unanswerable questions has authority they cannot match.
23: A direct revelation: "The same day Sadducees came to him, who say that there is no resurrection, and they asked him a question." The Sadducees — who accepted only the Pentateuch and denied resurrection — present the resurrection-dilemma. The Christological context: Jesus is himself the resurrection (John 11:25) and is about to demonstrate it. The one being questioned about resurrection will be raised from the dead within the week. The question is asked in the presence of its answer.
24: A revelation of God: "Saying, 'Teacher, Moses said, If a man dies having no children, his brother must marry the widow and raise up offspring for his brother.'" The levirate marriage law (Deuteronomy 25:5-6) is the Sadducean setup for the absurd scenario of Matthew 22:25-28. The Christological bearing: they use Mosaic law to argue against resurrection, but Jesus will use Mosaic law to argue for it (Matthew 22:31-32: Exodus 3:6). The same Torah contains both the levirate law and the resurrection-implication; Jesus reads it with more depth than those who reject the resurrection.
25: A revelation of God: "Now there were seven brothers among us. The first married and died, and having no offspring left his wife to his brother." The hypothetical chain of seven levirate marriages — designed to make resurrection seem absurd — is the Sadducean thought experiment. The Christological response will not engage the hypothetical directly but will expose the faulty premise: resurrection life is not the continuation of earthly categories.
26: A revelation of God: "So too the second and third, down to the seventh." The escalation to seven (the number of completeness) makes the hypothetical maximally absurd. The Christological response will show that the absurdity is created by assuming resurrection = resuscitation. The new creation body is not constrained by the institutions of the old creation.
27: A revelation of God: "After them all, the woman died." The woman's death after all seven husbands completes the hypothetical. The Sadducean question assumes the woman's resurrection entails a legal conflict: which husband? The Christological answer will dissolve the conflict by dissolving the premise.
28: A revelation of God: "In the resurrection, therefore, of the seven, whose wife will she be? For they all had her." The "therefore" of the Sadducean question is their logical conclusion: resurrection creates an insoluble legal problem. The Christological reply will expose that the "therefore" rests on a misunderstanding of what resurrection is.
29: A direct revelation: "But Jesus answered them, 'You are wrong, because you know neither the Scriptures nor the power of God.'" The double charge — wrong about Scripture and wrong about divine power — is the Christological authority of the one who knows both. The Sadducees' error is not merely hermeneutical but theological: they have limited God's power to what they know from earthly experience. The one who will rise from the dead tells them their limits.
30: A direct revelation: "For in the resurrection they neither marry nor are given in marriage, but are like angels in heaven." The resurrection state is not the extension of earthly institutions but a new mode of existence. The Christological bearing: the one who will be the first to be raised (1 Corinthians 15:20) announces the shape of resurrection life. Marriage belongs to the old creation order (Genesis 2:18-25); the new creation involves a different mode of communal existence. The Sadducean hypothetical dissolves because its premise (resurrection = earthly life continued) is false.
31: A direct revelation: "And as for the resurrection of the dead, have you not read what was said to you by God..." The "said to you by God" (to rhēthen hymin hypo tou theou) is the Christological reading of Scripture: these words in Exodus are not merely historical record but divine address to every hearer in every generation. The same God who spoke to Moses speaks through the text to those who read it. The living Scripture addresses the living community.
32: A direct revelation: "'I am the God of Abraham, and the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob'? He is not God of the dead, but of the living." The Christological argument from Exodus 3:6 is logical and theological: God says "I am" (present tense) the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob — who had been dead for centuries when Moses heard this. If God is their God in the present tense, they must be alive. God is in a living relationship with them; therefore they live. The one who makes this argument will himself be alive after death to confirm it.
33: A revelation of God: "And when the crowd heard it, they were astonished at his teaching." The crowd's astonishment at the resurrection-argument is the Christological effect of teaching with authority. The Sadducees' challenge has been turned into an argument for resurrection from within the Pentateuch they accepted. The one who teaches like this teaches not as a scribe but as the one who gave the Scriptures and knows their deepest intent.
34: A revelation of God: "But when the Pharisees heard that he had silenced the Sadducees, they gathered together." The Pharisees' regrouping after the Sadducees' failure is the Christological relay of opposition: each group takes a turn testing Jesus and failing. The gathering of the Pharisees after the Sadducees' defeat is the sustained opposition that will end only at the cross.
35: A revelation of God: "And one of them, a lawyer, asked him a question to test him." The lawyer's question — "to test him" (peirazōn, the temptation-word) — is the third Christological test of the Jerusalem week, following the Caesar-question and the resurrection-question. The testing continues; the one being tested is the same one who passed the wilderness test and every subsequent test.
36: A revelation of God: "Teacher, which is the great commandment in the Law?" The greatest-commandment question was a live rabbinic debate. The Christological significance: Jesus will give an answer that is not merely the best of the competing options but the interpretive key to the entire Torah. His answer does not choose between candidates but identifies the structure that holds all commandments together.
37: A direct revelation: "And he said to him, 'You shall love the Lord your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your mind.'" The citation of Deuteronomy 6:5 (the Shema's love-command) as the first and great commandment is the Christological affirmation of Israel's central confession. The one who cites "love the Lord your God" is himself the object of that love — the one in whom "the Lord your God" has become present. Loving God with all one's heart, soul, and mind is, in Matthew's Christology, loving the Son through whom the Father is made known.
38: A direct revelation: "This is the great and first commandment." The identification of the love-commandment as "great and first" (megalē kai prōtē) establishes it as the foundation from which all other commandments are derived and interpreted. The Christological bearing: the entire moral teaching of Jesus throughout the Gospel (the Sermon on the Mount, the community discourse, the conflict stories) is the explication of this first commandment — love for God that takes form in concrete human relationships.
39: A direct revelation: "And a second is like it: You shall love your neighbor as yourself." The citation of Leviticus 19:18 as the second commandment "like" the first establishes the inseparability of love for God and love for neighbor. The Christological integration: the one who embodies both — who loves the Father perfectly and gives himself for his neighbors completely — is the one who cites both as the summary of Torah. The two-commandment summary is the description of Jesus' own life.
40: A direct revelation: "On these two commandments depend all the Law and the Prophets." The hanging of "all the Law and the Prophets" on the two love-commandments is the Christological hermeneutic that has governed Jesus' teaching throughout Matthew. The Law and the Prophets (Matthew 5:17: "I have come not to abolish them but to fulfill them") are fulfilled by the one who loves God perfectly and loves his neighbor to death. The cross is the supreme act of the double love-commandment.
41: A direct revelation: "Now while the Pharisees were gathered together, Jesus asked them a question." The Christological reversal: after three tests in which the Pharisees tested Jesus, Jesus now asks the definitive question. The questioner becomes the questioned. The one who has answered every test now poses the test that cannot be answered from within their framework.
42: A direct revelation: "Saying, 'What do you think about the Christ? Whose son is he?' They said to him, 'The son of David.'" The Pharisees' answer — "Son of David" — is correct as far as it goes (the Davidic descent of the Messiah is the standard expectation). The Christological trap: "Son of David" is true but insufficient. The Messiah is more than David's descendant; he is David's Lord. The question will expose the inadequacy of their messianic categories.
43: A direct revelation: "He said to them, 'How is it then that David, in the Spirit, calls him Lord, saying...'" The "in the Spirit" (en pneumati) identifies the source of Ps 110's authority: David did not write from political imagination but under divine inspiration. The Christological hermeneutic: Scripture is Spirit-inspired, which means its meaning exceeds what the human author consciously intended. David in the Spirit wrote about the Messiah he could not fully see — and Jesus, who is the Messiah, can read what David's Spirit-given words point toward.
45: A direct revelation: "If then David calls him Lord, how is he his son?" The logical question — if David (the ancestor) calls the Messiah "Lord" (the superior), how can the Messiah be merely David's descendant? — is the Christological demonstration of the inadequacy of the "Son of David" category alone. The Messiah must be more than human descent can explain. He must be, as the Nicene Creed will say, "God from God" — the divine Son who entered David's line, not merely the human product of that line.
46: A direct revelation: "And no one was able to answer him a word, nor from that day did anyone dare to ask him any more questions." The silencing of all questioners — Pharisees, Herodians, Sadducees, lawyers — is the Christological authority-demonstration of the Jerusalem controversy. He has answered every test perfectly, exposed every trap, and posed one question that no one can answer. The silence before the Son of God is the prelude to the trial where they will finally succeed — not by answering him but by bribing Judas and manipulating Pilate.
